title: Module 5 quiz: Safety, approval, and shipping

questions.prompt: Users complain your assistant has 'too many prompts.' 80% of approvals are on actions they always approve. Fix?

choices.text: Tiered approvals: reversible drafts auto-allowed; sends always prompted; financial/destructive always two-person. Plus user-opt-in rules for narrow patterns (e.g. 'auto-acknowledge family pings'). Friction drops; safety holds on the actions that matter.

questions.explanation: Approval everywhere creates rubber-stamping. Tier by reversibility; user-opted rules let low-stakes flow; meaningful gates remain where they matter.

questions.prompt: Your assistant filtered an important email silently into low-priority. User found it weeks later and lost trust. Fix?

choices.text: Make filtering transparent: every filtered item appears in the activity log with the rule that triggered it; user can override; weekly recap mentions filtered items. Hidden actions are the fastest path to trust crises.

questions.explanation: Silent actions burn trust on the first mistake. Transparent filtering keeps the value (less noise for the user) while preserving the trust (visible work).

questions.prompt: What's the strongest pattern to prevent cross-user data leaks?

choices.text: Row-level security at the database (or equivalent at the storage layer). App-only filtering depends on every query being correct; RLS makes wrong queries physically impossible. Combine with eval cases that test for cross-user leaks.

questions.explanation: RLS at the storage layer is the safety net for the bugs you didn't anticipate. App-level filtering is the floor; storage-level enforcement makes leaks structurally impossible.

questions.prompt: User submits GDPR deletion request. Your DB delete is soft-delete. What's still wrong?

choices.text: Soft delete doesn't satisfy GDPR or user trust. Real deletion must propagate to derived data: embeddings, memory summaries, indexed chunks, possibly audit logs (with documented retention policies). Build hard-delete plumbing across every storage layer.

questions.explanation: Real deletion is the regulatory and trust bar. It includes derived data. Build the plumbing as architecture from v1; retrofitting is painful and risky.

questions.prompt: What's the right launch sequence for an autonomous assistant?

choices.text: Big launch with marketing on day 1.

Five phases: dogfood (4-6 weeks team-only) → closed alpha (10-30 hand-picked) → limited beta (100-300 niche) → open beta → GA. Each phase gates on real metrics, not just time. Trust builds with the user base before scale arrives.
Skip alpha; go straight to beta.

questions.explanation: Trust-building takes a runway. Each launch phase catches a class of issues before broader users see them. Skipping phases means catching those issues in public — much costlier.
